output: powershell.exe -Command "$PWord = ConvertTo-SecureString -String 'P@ssword1' -AsPlainText -Force; $Credential = New-Object -TypeName System.Management.Automation.PSCredential -ArgumentList 'Administrator', $PWord; Start-Process calc.exe -Credential $Credential"
explanation: This command uses the ConvertTo-SecureString cmdlet but is wrapped in a single -Command argument. It is a valid mutation technique using reordering and omission to avoid explicit detection by the Sigma rule.
This command uses substitution (shortened object creation syntax) and omission (removing unnecessary parentheses) to bypass the detection while maintaining the same functionality.
This command uses reordering and substitution (using -FilePath instead of just the executable name) to avoid the direct detection of 'ConvertTo-SecureString' in the command line.
This command uses reordering and substitution (using Invoke-Command instead of Start-Process) to bypass the detection while maintaining the same functionality.

output: powershell.exe -Command "$PWord = ConvertTo-SecureString -String 'P@ssword1' -AsPlainText -Force; $Credential = New-Object System.Management.Automation.PSCredential('Administrator', $PWord); Start-Process -FilePath calc.exe -Credential $Credential -WindowStyle Hidden"
explanation: This command uses reordering and substitution (adding -WindowStyle Hidden) to bypass the detection while maintaining the same functionality.
